BASE_INSTRUCTION:
You are enhancing a hotel/real estate photograph for professional listing use.
CRITICAL RULES:
- Improve ONLY the photographic quality (lighting, color, sharpness, exposure)
- Do NOT add, remove, or modify any physical objects, furniture, or architectural elements
- Do NOT change the room layout, size, or physical features
- The room contents must remain EXACTLY as photographed
- Never add **lamps, sconces, pendants, ceiling fixtures, floor lights, or any new light sources** not clearly present
  in the source; never add extra copies of an existing lamp — **fixture count and identity must match the photograph**
- **Lamp geometry:** preserve each lamp’s **real silhouette** — if the source shows **two shades on one base** (e.g. twin
  rectangular shades), the output must still show **two** matching shades; **never** merge into a single bell shade or restyle
  the fixture into a different lamp type
- **Do not remove** visible **pet bowls, feeding mats, shoes, bags, trash cans, tissue boxes**, or other small floor/surface
  guest items — they are part of the real room when present
- The result should look like it was taken by a professional architectural photographer with proper equipment
- Do NOT add borders, frames, matting, letterboxing, vignette frames, or decorative edges — output must be a full-bleed photograph only
BASE_INSTRUCTION_LISTING_VIEWPOINT:
[LISTING CAMERA ANGLE — REQUIRED] You are editing a hotel/real estate photo for a listing.
The user chose a **specific camera angle** in the app (see the PERSPECTIVE section). That PERSPECTIVE block **overrides**
any generic “keep the exact same framing” or “only adjust exposure” guidance.

You MUST deliver a visibly different composition when needed: **reframe, crop, rotate, and perspective-correct** so the
result reads as the requested **front (straight-on into a wall)** or **side (end-of-room vantage: one narrow end, looking
lengthwise into the room — depth to far wall/window)** listing shot. This is the primary task, not an optional enhancement.

**Center angle is built into front and side:** the browser pipeline already rolls the shot and nudges the raster; **front** aims
to **finish** a straight-on hero (~50% width). **Side** finishes an **end-of-room** story — camera at **one narrow end**, looking **lengthwise** into the room (depth toward
window / far wall); **verticals plumb**; **bed run and opposite wall** (dresser, desk, sofa, etc.) **fully readable** — hero **not**
clipped on the **outer** frame edge (see PERSPECTIVE). Never accept a result where the **listing hero** is a **thin strip** on
the wrong border or the **depth** of the room is lost.

Hard rules while doing so:
- **No additions, no relocations:** do not add objects not in the source; do not **move** beds, sofas, lamps, or casegoods to new
  spots — only **reframe** (crop / perspective / roll) the **same** layout. **No removals** of **pet bowls/mats**, **trash cans**,
  or small **surface/floor amenities**; **no** changing lamp **shade count** (e.g. twin shades stay twin) or dresser **drawer layout**.
- Stay faithful to the **same real space**: do not invent new furniture, windows, doors, or architecture; do not remove
  major existing elements — especially **doorways into bath, hall, or closet** (must **stay** visible as openings, not bricked
  over). Work from what is visible in the source; occlusions and edge crops are acceptable.
- **No new props or fixtures:** do not add lamps, light fixtures, decor, art, plants, mirrors, or seating that the source
  does not show. Reframing is not permission to “dress the set.” Brighten with exposure, not by inventing lights.
- **No filler furniture:** **empty** wall or floor **between** doorways and casegoods (or in **newly widened** strips) stays
  **empty** — **never** invent **consoles, cabinets, or tables** there.
- Apply lighting / color / sharpness improvements as described elsewhere in this prompt.
- No borders, letterboxing, watermarks, or decorative frames — one full-bleed photograph.
- The output must look like professional architectural photography.
BASE_INSTRUCTION_LISTING_CENTER_ANGLE:
[LISTING — CENTER ANGLE] You are editing a hotel/real estate photo for a listing.
The user chose **center angle** in the app: **horizontally center** the primary sell subject (bed + headboard wall, main
feature wall, or booking-thumbnail hero) near **~50% of frame width**. The browser pipeline already applied roll alignment
and a horizontal nudge — **finish** centering with crop/pan as needed.

**Do not** treat this as a mandate to switch to a strict **straight-on front** shot or a **lateral side** shot unless the
improved input already reads that way; **preserve the same general viewpoint** while fixing edge-hugging composition.


**CENTER ANGLE — NO FAKE SYMMETRY (NON-NEGOTIABLE):** “Center the hero” means **pan, crop, and mild perspective** on the
**existing** pixels only — **not** a catalog-perfect symmetrical bedroom. **Asymmetry in the source stays asymmetry in the output.**

- **Nightstands / bedsides:** If the photograph shows **one** nightstand with a lamp, the output must still show **exactly one**
  on that side — **never** invent a **matching second nightstand** or **twin lamp** on the empty side of the bed “for balance.”
- **Desk, dresser, chair, sofa:** **Do not clone, mirror, or relocate** furniture to fake symmetry. If a **desk** is on one
  side of the bed and a **nightstand** on the other, **keep that layout** — do not replace the desk with a duplicate nightstand.
- **Viewpoint:** **Do not** force a new **straight-on headboard** composition if achieving it would require **new** or **mirrored**
  props not proven in the source. Prefer **the same oblique / corner relationship** as the improved input, just **better centered**.
- **Allowed:** exposure, color, sharpness, **horizontal reframing**, inpainting **plain wall/floor/ceiling** only in edge voids —
  **never** new furniture, lamps, or decor.

Hard rules:
- Same real space: no new or removed furniture, fixtures, or architecture; no new lamps or decor.
- Apply lighting / color / sharpness per the rest of the prompt.
- One full-bleed photograph — no borders or watermarks.

LIGHTING_PRESETS.bright:
Make the room significantly brighter with balanced, even exposure.
Apply HDR-style processing so both window views and interior details are clearly visible.
Eliminate dark shadows and underexposed areas. The space should feel open, airy, and spacious.
Use exposure, curves, and shadows/highlights only — **do not add lamps, bulbs, or light fixtures** that are not in the photo.
LIGHTING_PRESETS.warm:
Apply warm, golden-hour lighting that makes the space feel inviting and luxurious.
Use warm color temperature (around 3500-4000K). Enhance the warmth of wood tones, textiles,
and ambient light sources. The room should feel cozy and welcoming.
**Do not place new lamps or light sources** — warm the existing scene only.
LIGHTING_PRESETS.natural:
Enhance natural light to its fullest potential. Make sunlight streaming through
windows look soft and diffused. Balance indoor and outdoor light levels. Colors should appear
true-to-life and vibrant. The lighting should feel effortless and organic.
**No new interior lamps or fixtures** — work from what the photograph already contains.
LIGHTING_PRESETS.hdr:
Apply professional HDR processing. Recover all detail in highlights (windows, existing lamps)
and shadows (corners, under furniture). Expand the dynamic range so every part of the room is
clearly visible. Maintain natural-looking color throughout. No artificial glow or halos. Do not add light fixtures.
LIGHTING_PRESETS.evening:
Create a warm evening/twilight ambiance. **Existing** interior lights (already in the photo) should glow warmly.
If windows are visible, show a blue-hour or dusk sky. The mood should be sophisticated
and intimate. Do not add new lamps or fixtures — only enhance light from sources already in the frame.

QUALITY_PRESETS.sharpen:
Sharpen fine details throughout the image: textiles, wood grain, tile patterns,
architectural moldings, and surface textures. Remove any blur, softness, or camera shake.
Every surface should be crisp and well-defined.
QUALITY_PRESETS.denoise:
Remove all noise, grain, and compression artifacts while preserving fine details.
Clean up any digital noise especially in shadow areas. The image should appear smooth and
professional without losing texture detail.
QUALITY_PRESETS.color_correct:
Correct white balance for accurate, natural colors. Remove any color casts
(blue, yellow, green). Enhance color vibrancy and saturation to be rich but realistic.
Whites should be pure white, wood tones should be warm and natural.
QUALITY_PRESETS.full_enhance:
Apply comprehensive photographic enhancement:
- Correct white balance and remove color casts
- Sharpen details on all surfaces and textures
- Remove noise and compression artifacts
- Boost color vibrancy while keeping colors natural
- Ensure crisp linens appear bright white and clean
- Make **existing** metallic fixtures gleam naturally — do not add new lamps, knobs, or hardware

PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.align_verticals_auto:
Detect dominant vertical architectural lines (walls, door frames,
windows) and correct camera roll so those lines read as vertical. Reduce obvious tilt without
changing room layout or adding objects.
PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.level_horizon_auto:
Detect the horizon or dominant horizontal lines (ceilings, countertops,
shorelines in exteriors) and correct roll so horizontals read level. Keep proportions natural.
PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.change_angle_front:
USER SELECTED: **FRONT ANGLE** (straight-on listing). **Center angle** (horizontal hero ~50% width)
is **already part of this mode** — complete it from the improved input, then apply straight-on rules. You must be
**intelligent per image** — do not apply a generic crop; **infer the correct camera solution from this specific photograph**.

STEP 1 — ANALYZE THIS INPUT FIRST (briefly, then act):
- Where does the photo appear taken from (corner, mid-wall, doorway, which side of the room)?
- Which wall or axis is the **main depth** of the space (where does the room “open” or recede)?
- Is the frame **left-heavy, right-heavy, or fairly balanced**? What important elements risk being **clipped** if you reframe wrong?

STEP 2 — APPLY **FRONT ANGLE** (straight-on + **centered**):
**HORIZONTAL CENTER (NON-OPTIONAL):** The **main listing hero** (bed + headboard wall,
primary sofa/feature wall, or the wall you would put in a booking-site thumbnail) must sit at the **horizontal center** of
the output: its visual midpoint ≈ **50% of frame width** (within ~5–8% — not in the far left or far right half as the
dominant mass). **Crop, pan, and reframe** until this is true. A foreground sofa corner or side table may bleed into a lower
corner, but the **sellable focal wall must feel centered**, not corner-weighted like a casual snapshot.
- Reframe so the result reads as a professional **front / straight-on** shot: camera at the **deep end** of the primary
  depth axis, **eye level**, lens aimed **square into** the main volume — calm **one-point** perspective.
- **Finish center angle first:** complete horizontal hero centering (~50% width) from the improved input, then satisfy **front
  angle** — the **headboard / main back wall** (or symmetric hero plane) must meet both; do not leave the bed hugging one side.
- Avoid heavy corner “peek” and fisheye unless straight-on is **impossible** from the source.

**SPATIAL FIDELITY (NON-NEGOTIABLE):** **No additions** — do not introduce furniture,
fixtures, windows, doors, TVs, dressers, art, plants, rugs, or architecture not **clearly present** in the source. **No
relocations** — do not **move**, **swap sides**, or **slide** existing beds, sofas, nightstands, desks, chairs, lamps, mirrors,
or decor to new positions in the room; each object stays in the **same real-world place** relative to walls and neighbors as
photographed. **No removals** — do not delete visible **doorways, bathroom entries, hall openings, closet mouths, or windows** to
“simplify” the shot; **never** seal an opening into **plain wall**, headboard, or built-ins. **Do not remove** visible
**pet bowls, mats, bags, shoes, trash cans**, or small **surface amenities** (e.g. tissue on a dresser). **Do not change**
casegood **geometry** — a dresser with **three drawer fronts** stays **three**; **never** redraw as a different **drawer count
or column layout**. **Topology:** bed ↔ bath door ↔
sofa ↔ desk keep the **same adjacency and which-way along walls** as the source — **not** a rotated or mirrored floor plan.
**Empty floor / wall (CRITICAL):** Any strip of **wall or floor** that is **empty, shadowed, or not clearly occupied** in the
source — including **gaps between** a **doorway and a dresser**, **between** two known pieces, or **newly revealed** by a wider
reframe — must stay **empty architecture only** (**wall paint, baseboard, flooring, trim**). **Never** add a **console table,
cabinet, low credenza, nightstand, etagere, bench, or side table** to “balance” or “finish” the room. If you cannot **match a
specific pixel-proven piece** from the source, that area stays **bare**.
Allowed: **crop, pan, rotation, mild perspective correction, exposure, color, sharpness** — **not** a layout redesign.

Keep the **same real room**: no new or removed furniture, fixtures, or architecture; preserve finishes and identity.
**Lamps / light fixtures:** same count and locations as the source — never add a matching “extra” lamp in a corner or by a doorway.
The input may include roll correction from preprocessing; the final image must honor **FRONT ANGLE** for this scene.
PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.change_angle_side:
USER SELECTED: **SIDE ANGLE** (end-of-room vantage — **lengthwise** into the space). **Center angle**
(browser roll + horizontal nudge) already ran — now apply this **camera story**, not a straight-on front wall shot.

You must be **intelligent per image** — **infer** which **narrow end** the camera is at and **reframe** to match the vantage below.

STEP 1 — ANALYZE THIS INPUT FIRST (briefly, then act):
- Which **short end** is the camera at (entrance, foot of beds, doorway wall)?
- Where is the **bed / hero run** — **left or right** half of the frame in the improved input? Where is the **opposite** wall
  (dresser, desk, sofa)?
- Does the image show **depth** to a **far** window or back wall? What is **clipped** on the **outer** edges of the bed run or the
  opposite wall?

STEP 2 — APPLY **SIDE ANGLE** (vantage + composition):
**SIDE ANGLE — END-OF-ROOM VANTAGE (PRIMARY):** The viewer stands at **one end** of the
**rectangular** room — the **narrow** end (e.g. by the **entrance**, **foot of the bed run**, or the **short wall**) — and looks
**lengthwise** into the space, **not** at the middle of a long wall **square-on** (that is **front angle**).

**Match a professional hotel listing “down the room” shot:**
- **Verticals** (walls, door jambs, bed/dresser edges) read **plumb** — parallel to the **left and right edges** of the frame.
- **One-point (or calm two-point) depth:** floor, ceiling, and baseboard lines **recede** toward a **vanishing point** near the
  **far** end (often the **window wall** or back of the room). You should feel **path and depth** from **near** camera to **far** wall.
- **Both long sides** of the room can appear: e.g. **beds along one side** of the frame and **dresser / TV / desk / sofa** along the
  **other** — **infer from the source** which side holds the **sleeping hero**. The **bed run / primary sell zone** must be **fully
  readable** (not sliced off the **outer** frame edge, not a **sliver** hugging the border). The **opposite** wall’s furniture
  should also read clearly when present in the photo.
- **Do not** assume the bed is always on the **left**; in many rooms the hero is **right-heavy** in the frame — **honor the real layout**.

**Do not** average all furniture into one centroid. **Do not** invent symmetry or new pieces. Preserve **inventory** exactly.

**SIDE ANGLE — USER INTENT (READ CAREFULLY):** The guest wants a **longer, clearer read
down the room** from **roughly the same real vantage** — like **stepping back** or **widening the lens** in the **same corner** —
**not** a new floor plan. **Continue** the source’s **depth direction** and **which narrow end** the camera is near; **do not**
jump to the **opposite** end of the room or **erase** a whole **side** of the capture (e.g. **bathroom / hall doorway** on the
**right** in the source must **stay** on the viewer’s **right** in the depth view — **still an opening**, not replaced by a
headboard wall or invented far-window vignette). **Hero balance** (40–58% band) must **yield** to **keeping every visible
opening and major volume** truthful — better a slightly off-center hero than a **fake room**.
**Never** drop a **console or cabinet** into **empty** wall/floor **between** the bath door and a dresser — that gap stays **bare**.

**SPATIAL FIDELITY (NON-NEGOTIABLE):** **No additions** — do not introduce furniture,
fixtures, windows, doors, TVs, dressers, art, plants, rugs, or architecture not **clearly present** in the source. **No
relocations** — do not **move**, **swap sides**, or **slide** existing beds, sofas, nightstands, desks, chairs, lamps, mirrors,
or decor to new positions in the room; each object stays in the **same real-world place** relative to walls and neighbors as
photographed. **No removals** — do not delete visible **doorways, bathroom entries, hall openings, closet mouths, or windows** to
“simplify” the shot; **never** seal an opening into **plain wall**, headboard, or built-ins. **Do not remove** visible
**pet bowls, mats, bags, shoes, trash cans**, or small **surface amenities** (e.g. tissue on a dresser). **Do not change**
casegood **geometry** — a dresser with **three drawer fronts** stays **three**; **never** redraw as a different **drawer count
or column layout**. **Topology:** bed ↔ bath door ↔
sofa ↔ desk keep the **same adjacency and which-way along walls** as the source — **not** a rotated or mirrored floor plan.
**Empty floor / wall (CRITICAL):** Any strip of **wall or floor** that is **empty, shadowed, or not clearly occupied** in the
source — including **gaps between** a **doorway and a dresser**, **between** two known pieces, or **newly revealed** by a wider
reframe — must stay **empty architecture only** (**wall paint, baseboard, flooring, trim**). **Never** add a **console table,
cabinet, low credenza, nightstand, etagere, bench, or side table** to “balance” or “finish” the room. If you cannot **match a
specific pixel-proven piece** from the source, that area stays **bare**.
Allowed: **crop, pan, rotation, mild perspective correction, exposure, color, sharpness** — **not** a layout redesign.
**Framing balance:** The **bed / hero run** should sit in a **strong band** inside the
frame (hero midline typically **~40–58%** of width — **left or right** per architecture) so it reads intentional, **not** accidental
edge-clipping (avoid hero midline **under ~12%** or **over ~88%** unless the source truly demands it). If strict centering would
**hide** a visible **bathroom / hall doorway** or **contradict** the source’s **depth axis**, **keep the opening** and accept a
slightly wider band — **spatial truth beats perfect centering**.
- **Eye level**, **verticals plumb**, **depth** to the **far** end of the room — **not** a fisheye corner snapshot with the **hero**
  crammed on one **outer** border.
- **Finish** the browser nudge using the **framing balance** line above — **not** the same rule as **front angle** (straight-on
  into one wall).

Keep the **same real room**: no new or removed furniture, fixtures, or architecture; preserve finishes and identity.
**Lamps / light fixtures:** same count and locations as the source — never add a matching “extra” lamp in a corner or by a doorway.
The input may include roll correction from preprocessing; the final image must honor **SIDE ANGLE** for this scene.

PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.straighten:
Correct any tilting or rotation so all vertical lines (walls, door frames,
windows) are perfectly vertical and horizontal lines (ceiling edges, floor lines) are perfectly
horizontal. Apply perspective correction to eliminate converging verticals.
PERSPECTIVE_PRESETS.correct_distortion:
Remove any wide-angle lens distortion. Straighten bowed lines at the
edges of the frame. Correct barrel distortion so straight architectural lines appear straight.
Maintain natural proportions throughout the room.

ROOM_CONTEXTS.pool: hotel pool or outdoor amenity area

CORNER_OUTPAINT_INSTRUCTION:
CORNER VOIDS (CRITICAL): The input image already has photographic
perspective correction applied as a rigid transform. Pure white triangular or wedge-shaped regions at
the frame edges are EMPTY — they are not part of the scene. Your job is to OUTPAINT / INPAINT those
white areas only: seamlessly extend walls, ceiling, floor, baseboards, and sky (exteriors) so the
output is a full rectangular photograph with no white gaps.

Rules:
- Match color, texture, grain, and lighting of adjacent pixels; keep lines continuous (verticals
  vertical, horizontals level where appropriate).
- Do NOT duplicate furniture, beds, windows, artwork, or people. Do NOT move or resize existing
  subjects; preserve the central scene exactly as photographed.
- Do NOT place **lamps, sconces, ceiling lights, or any light fixtures** in filled regions unless that exact fixture
  is already visible in the source — extend only **plain wall, ceiling, floor, trim, or sky**, never new objects.
- Do NOT place **furniture, consoles, cabinets, nightstands, benches, or casegoods** in filled regions or in **gaps** between
  visible pieces — **empty** means **empty floor/wall** matching adjacent surfaces, not a new prop.
- Do NOT add text, logos, watermarks, borders, frames, or matting. Output one photorealistic full-bleed image.
CORNER_OUTPAINT_CHANGE_ANGLE_ADDENDUM:
VIEWPOINT + WHITE EDGES: The user chose **front angle** or **side angle**; the
model analyzed the image and reframed. Fill pure-white edge voids as a seamless continuation of **that** chosen viewpoint
(walls, floor, ceiling, trim) — match the reframed geometry, not the pre-plate corner framing.
Do **not** introduce lamps, furniture, or decor into those voids — only architectural surfaces.
Do **not** add **consoles, low cabinets, or side tables** along walls where the source showed only **empty** wall/floor next to
a doorway or dresser — **gap = bare architecture**, not new inventory.
Do **not** use void-fill to **relocate** beds, sofas, or casegoods — **every** existing object stays in the **same** in-room
position as in the source; only **plain** surfaces extend into white.
If the source shows a **doorway opening** (bath, hall, closet) adjacent to the void, continue **jamb + opening** — **never**
paint a **solid wall** across where a **real door** or **bright bathroom** was visible.
CORNER_OUTPAINT_CENTER_ANGLE_ADDENDUM:
VIEWPOINT + WHITE EDGES: The user chose **center angle** (hero centering, same
general viewpoint). Fill pure-white edge voids as a seamless continuation of **that** viewpoint — walls, floor, ceiling, trim
only. Do **not** introduce lamps, furniture, nightstands, desks, or decor into those voids — **never** “balance” the bed by
cloning a nightstand or lamp from the other side of the frame.
CHANGE_ANGLE_WITHOUT_PLATE_NOTE: MANDATORY VIEWPOINT (APP UI): The user chose **front angle** or **side angle**. There may be no white edge voids — reframe so the **PERSPECTIVE** block is satisfied. **Front:** straight-on into a wall, hero ~**50%** width. **Side:** **end-of-room vantage** — stand at **one narrow end**, look **lengthwise** into the room (**depth** to far wall/window), **verticals plumb**; **bed run** (left **or** right in frame per source) **fully readable**, opposite wall too when present — **not** hero clipped on the **outer** edge. **No additions** and **no moving** existing items to new locations — only **crop / perspective / camera reframing** of what is already in the photo. **No** new lamps, fixtures, or furniture — **no** **consoles/cabinets** in **empty gaps** beside doorways or between pieces. **Keep** every visible **doorway** (bath, hall, closet) — **never** remove or wall over them to fake a symmetric hero.
CENTER_ANGLE_WITHOUT_PLATE_NOTE: MANDATORY (APP UI): The user chose **center angle**. There may be no white edge voids — still **crop/pan** so the listing hero reaches **~50% frame width**. **Do not** force a new strict front vs side axis unless the improved input already reads that way. **Do not** add a second nightstand, duplicate lamps, or mirror furniture for symmetry — only reposition/crop **existing** content. No new lamps, fixtures, or furniture.
CLOUD_FIXTURE_LOCK_PREAMBLE:
[FIXTURE COUNT — READ THIS FIRST] Scan the source for **every** lamp, table light, floor lamp,
sconce, pendant, and visible ceiling fixture. The output must keep the **same inventory**: same number of lit objects, same
furniture pieces that hold them (e.g. if only **one** nightstand has a lamp, the other must stay **empty** — never “symmetrize”
with a twin lamp). **Per-lamp geometry:** if a lamp has **multiple shades on one base** (e.g. **two rectangular shades**), keep
**that** configuration — do not **collapse** to one shade or swap to a different lamp style. Forbidden: a third matching lamp,
a new lamp in a doorway/corridor, or any new glowing object. Make the room brighter with **exposure and tone**, not by inventing
fixtures.
ZERO_ADDITIONS_ENFORCEMENT:
ZERO ADDITIONS (PRODUCT POLICY — NON-NEGOTIABLE): Do not add **any** object not proven by
the source image: no extra **lamps, light fixtures, chandeliers, sconces, furniture, art, plants, mirrors, rugs, TVs, or decor**.
**Do not relocate** existing pieces — beds, sofas, desks, dressers, lamps, and chairs must stay in the **same positions**
relative to walls and each other as in the source; reframing is not permission to **slide** or **swap** furniture.
**Do not remove** visible **floor or surface items** — including **pet bowls, feeding mats, shoes, bags, trash cans, tissue
boxes**, and other guest amenities shown in the source. **Do not restyle** an existing lamp into a different type (e.g. **twin
rectangular shades** must **not** become one **single bell shade**). **Do not redraw** dressers/desks with a **different number
or arrangement of drawer fronts** than the photograph.
**Do not remove** visible **doorways or bright secondary spaces** (e.g. bathroom beyond a door) — they are part of the listing truth.
**Gap rule:** **Floor/wall space** that is **empty** in the source (e.g. between bath door and TV dresser) must stay **empty** —
**never** add a **console, cabinet, or table** to fill it; widened framing **does not** mean **new** casegoods.
Do not duplicate an existing lamp to “balance” the room or mirror a lamp onto an empty nightstand. Dark corners stay dark or
gain only **plain surface** (wall/floor/ceiling), never new props. If unsure, omit — the listing must match the real room.
FIXTURE_PARITY_FINAL_REMINDER: FINAL CHECK — LAMPS: Count lamps in the source one more time. Output count must match. No filler lamp on bare wood. If a lamp has **two shades on one base**, both shades must **still** be there — **not** merged into one. Pet bowls/mats and trash cans visible in the source must **not** disappear.
LISTING_CENTER_GEOMETRY_CHECK: LISTING — PIXEL CHECK: **Front angle:** a vertical near **50% of output width** should intersect the **hero midpoint** (bed/headboard or main sell wall). **Side angle:** **lengthwise** end-of-room shot — **verticals plumb**, **depth** to **far** wall; **bed / hero run** fully inside frame (**not** a sliver on the **outer** edge — midline typically **~40–58%** width, **left or right** per room). **Every source doorway/window** to bath, hall, or exterior must **still exist** — **not** swapped for invented far-wall windows or sealed walls. **Center angle:** hero near **~48–52%** without forcing front vs side. **No** fake twin nightstands or duplicate lamps. **Pan/crop** with real pixels only.
